steps.title: Fastest check: compare a table or graph
steps.content: For an equivalent-expression question, enter the original expression and the answer choices in a calculator table or graph. The correct choice must match for several inputs, not just one. The matching choice is A, $\frac{{h}^{12}}{{q}^{16}}$.

steps.title: Algebra check
steps.content: Use the work below to confirm the identity for every allowed input. This matters because one random matching input is only a quick screen, not a proof that two expressions are always equivalent.

steps.title: Apply the quotient rule for exponents
steps.content:
When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the exponents. Apply this separately to $h$ and $q$:
$$\frac{h^{16}}{h^4} = h^{16-4} = h^{12}$$
$$\frac{q^{8}}{q^{24}} = q^{8-24} = q^{-16}$$

steps.title: Rewrite with positive exponents
steps.content:
A negative exponent moves the factor to the denominator:
$$q^{-16} = \frac{1}{q^{16}}$$
So the expression simplifies to $\dfrac{h^{12}}{q^{16}}$.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — $\frac{{h}^{12}}{{q}^{16}}$: $h^{16-4} = h^{12}$ in the numerator; $q^{8-24} = q^{-16} = \frac{1}{q^{16}}$ in the denominator. Correct. ✓
  • Choice B — $\frac{{h}^{4}}{{q}^{3}}$: $h^{16} / h^4 = h^{12}$, not $h^4$; and $q^8 / q^{24} = q^{-16}$, not $q^{-3}$. Both exponents are wrong. ✗
  • Choice C — ${h}^{12}{q}^{16}$: Gets the $h$ exponent right but misplaces $q$ in the numerator instead of the denominator. ✗
  • Choice D — ${h}^{4}{q}^{3}$: Both exponents are wrong and $q$ is in the wrong position. ✗
That matches Choice A — $\frac{{h}^{12}}{{q}^{16}}$.
